summary: Gruber dismantles a New York Times piece by Randall Stross that argues AT&T's network isn't to blame for iPhone service problems — that the iPhone hardware itself is at fault. Gruber systematically attacks Stross's sources, noting one network testing firm counts AT&T as a client and another excludes iPhones from its data. He contrasts these with Consumer Reports surveys of actual customers ranking AT&T last. He argues that if the iPhone were truly at fault, the problems would be global rather than concentrated in U.S. metro areas, and would have been fixed by the third model year. Gruber concludes the simpler explanation — AT&T's network can't handle the capacity — is obviously correct.
key_points: Stross's primary source is a network testing firm that counts AT&T as a client but not Verizon, undermining its credibility as an independent arbiter
Consumer Reports ranked AT&T last based on actual customer experiences, contradicting the industry consultants Stross relies on
iPhone carriers in Europe and Canada enabled tethering immediately and don't have comparable service problems, suggesting the issue is AT&T-specific rather than iPhone-specific
The correlation between growing iPhone adoption and worsening AT&T service points to network capacity as the root cause
The argument that U.S. geography makes coverage harder doesn't hold up because the worst problems are in dense metro areas, not rural ones
money_quotes: Stross's primary source is Paul Carter, the president of Global Wireless Solutions, a network testing firm. Carter acknowledges that AT&T is one of his company's clients, and Verizon is not. I mean, come on.
Consumer Reports's rankings, which put AT&T last and Verizon first, were based on actual customer surveys. Actual customers reporting their actual experiences.
If it's the iPhone's fault, not AT&T's, why aren't iPhone users around the world having the same problems as those here in the U.S.?
If it's crummy phone networking hardware in the iPhone, how come it's still a problem in the iPhone's third model year? Stross's implication is that Apple is an incompetent hardware maker.

If it's the iPhone's fault, why have iPhone/AT&T reception problems gotten worse over time? Doesn't the correlation between the number of iPhones in use and the increase in complaints about AT&T strongly suggest the problem is network capacity?

On the other hand, we have the theory that AT&T's network is just fine because two network consulting companies say so, even though a Consumer Reports customer survey says otherwise, and it is the iPhone that is flawed, but the flaws are for some reason worse on AT&T than other carriers around the world, and just happen to be worse still in some cities than others, and Apple has been unwilling and/or unable to address these flaws in three model years.
I know which theory I'd put my money on.
key_insight: When one explanation requires a chain of unlikely coincidences and conflicted sources while the other is simple and consistent with customer experience, Occam's razor should settle it — AT&T's network couldn't handle the iPhone's demands.
